title: King prawn balti
subtitle: Fast, fragrant prawn curry in the Birmingham balti tradition
excerpt: King prawns cooked quickly in a spiced tomato and pepper sauce in a steel balti dish over high heat. The balti technique is defined by speed (the whole dish cooks in under 15 minutes once the sauce is ready) and by the heat of the steel pan, which gives the sauce a slight char and intensity. A Birmingham curry-mile original, now a staple across the British Indian menu.
sourceNotes: The balti curry style was developed in Birmingham's Sparkhill and Sparkbrook areas (the 'Balti Triangle') from the late 1970s. The term refers to the steel wok-like vessel in which the food is cooked and served. Widely adopted across British Indian menus by the 1990s, particularly with seafood.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the sauce base ahead. Add prawns and cook to order in 5 minutes.

content.text: The balti technique relies on a very hot pan and a short cook. Prawns go from raw to overcooked in about 4 minutes once heat is applied — they are ready when they have turned from grey-translucent to pink-opaque and just curled. If they continue past that point, they tighten further, release moisture, and become rubbery. Watch them, not the clock.

A traditional balti includes a combination of whole and ground spices added in stages: whole spices first in hot oil for fragrance, then ground spices with the tomato base. The peppers and onion are kept slightly firm rather than cooked to softness — the balti should have textural presence, not be a smooth sauce with prawns in it.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide heavy pan or wok over high heat until shimmering. Add the {{cumin-seeds}} and fry for 20 seconds. Add the {{onion}}, {{pepper-red}}, and {{pepper-green}} and cook for 5 minutes, stirring, until softened but still with some texture. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{chilli-green}}, {{turmeric}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, and {{chilli-powder}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and cook for 8 minutes until reduced to a thick, fragrant sauce.

Add the {{king-prawns}} to the sauce. Toss to coat and cook over high heat for 3 to 4 minutes, stirring frequently, until the prawns are pink and just cooked through. Stir in the {{garam-masala}} and season with salt. Scatter over fresh coriander if using. Serve immediately — balti is a dish that waits for no one.

items.fix: Prawns are done when they turn pink and just curl. Remove from heat as soon as that happens — carryover heat continues the cooking

items.cause: Prawns released moisture into a thin sauce
items.fix: Reduce the tomato sauce to a very thick paste before adding the prawns. The prawns release liquid; the sauce must be thick enough to absorb it without becoming thin

items.cause: Peppers and onion cooked too soft
items.fix: In balti cooking, vegetables should retain some bite. Remove from heat while still slightly firm — they will soften further in the sauce

items.symptom: Lacks the charred intensity of restaurant balti
items.fix: Get the pan as hot as possible before adding the cumin seeds. The characteristic intensity comes from cooking over very high heat throughout

content.text: The balti is one of the few distinctly British contributions to the global Indian restaurant tradition — the style was developed in Birmingham and spread outward to the rest of the UK before reaching the wider world. Balti houses in the Balti Triangle traditionally serve the food in the same steel vessel it was cooked in, directly on the table with naan for scooping. The prawn version is one of the most popular: the quick cook suits seafood well, and the high-heat charring of the sauce gives it a depth that slow-cooked prawn curries cannot achieve.
